body: Professional rationale: in ACT-based chronic pain group management, we distinguish 'capacity variability' (energy available on a given day) from 'pain variability' (symptom intensity). The baseline needs to adapt to the former. A rolling average is the right architectural choice here.
Adaptive baseline is merged and deployed to staging. Frank - can you review the 7-day window logic? The reset trigger at 10% deviation might be too sensitive for some participants.

Reviewed in staging. The 10% deviation trigger is appropriate. In my professional experience, a 10%+ shift in 24h activity is meaningful. One suggestion: add a 3-day dampening window before the baseline resets, so a single outlier day doesn't skew the average.

Adding verbal anchors to the NRS is standard practice in pain assessment - most validated instruments (BPI, PSQ-3) include these. Recommend: 0-1 'no pain', 2-3 'mild', 4-5 'moderate', 6-7 'severe', 8-10 'very severe / worst imaginable'. Farrar et al. 2001 supports a 2-point change as the MCID.

The order matters professionally. When we start with 'what's your pain today?' we anchor the participant in their symptom. When we start with 'what do you want to be doing?' we anchor them in their life. That's the difference between symptom management and Occupational Therapy.

Q1 Chapter Advisory item. When we log a symptom spike without context, we lose the occupational data. 'Pain 7/10 while folding laundry' is far more professionally useful than 'pain 7/10.' The 'what were you doing?' field is the Occupational Therapy differentiator.

Think of it like a participant's occupational profile at a glance. Instead of seeing 'avg pain: 5.2 | adherence: 68%', the therapist should see 'goal: return to cooking | goal: trail hiking 2x/week | goal: full work shift without rest breaks.' That's what guides the session.

Dr. Tran confirmed interest. Recommending we start with 3 OTs (myself, Marcus Yee, and one rotating) and 5-8 participants per Occupational Therapy. I'll draft the pilot protocol and submit to Sasha by June 30.

From a professional standpoint this matters a lot. Persons with central sensitization fatigue quickly with long forms and abandon them. A 4-step wizard with save-and-resume respects their cognitive budget. Please keep each step to one clear question cluster.

Wizard scaffold is up on staging with the progress counter. Save-and-resume persists to the encrypted draft store. Frank - can you sanity-check the step ordering (occupation goals -> activity baseline -> symptom history -> consent)?
Step order looks right - occupation first is exactly the framing we want. One tweak: move the consent step to the very end so it's the final affirmative action, not buried mid-flow.

Borg RPE is the right instrument here. The 6-20 scale maps to heart rate (roughly RPE x 10), so it lets us infer cardiovascular load without a wearable. For graded activity work, perceived exertion is often more actionable than rep count alone.

Root cause: the offline sync queue serialized timestamps without time zone, and replay choked on entries older than the token refresh window. Fix wraps replay in a try/catch with a dead-letter queue and forces token refresh first. PR open for review.

This is a hard blocker for expanding the Bitterroot pilot. Cached private data on a coach's or participant's device must be encrypted at rest per the privacy Security Rule technical safeguards. Let's pair the device key to the authenticated session and wipe the cache on logout.
Agreed this is P1. Sasha - can we get a quick security review of the proposed key-derivation approach before Omar starts? Want to make sure we're not reinventing a crypto primitive.

Reproduced with a deuteranopia simulator - the amber and red zones collapse into one hue. Beyond contrast, we should never encode pacing status by color alone. Adding a fill pattern plus a small icon per zone fixes both the color-blindness and the glanceability issue.

Bitterroot's EMR speaks FHIR R4, so a DocumentReference resource for the finished resource note is the cleanest path. Two requirements before we build: explicit participant consent for the push, and an audit log entry for every export. I'll confirm the consent language with Dr. Trần.

Picking this up. Planning a daily job that flags participants with zero activity events in a rolling 7-day window and adds a 'needs outreach' badge to the workload card. Frank - should a logged symptom entry (without an activity) count as activity for this filter?

First draft of the consent is done. It covers what we collect, how it's stored and encrypted, who can see it, and the right to withdraw and request deletion at any time. Sending to Dr. Trần for professional and legal review before it goes to the pilot cohort.

The framing of this email is therapeutic, not just informational. When a person opens it and the first thing they see is 'avg pain 6.1', it reinforces a deficit narrative. Lead with what they did - 'you completed 3 of 4 cooking sessions this week' - and let the pain trend support that story rather than headline it.
